name: From Ginnungagap to the First Humans

stages: Ymir (first giant) emerges from melting ice

description: Also known as 🌌 Norse Creation Myth.

keyMyths.title: The Void of Ginnungagap
keyMyths.description: In the beginning, there was only Ginnungagap, a vast, empty void. To the north lay Niflheim, a realm of ice and cold, and to the south, Muspelheim, a realm of fire and heat. The interaction of these realms within Ginnungagap initiated creation.

keyMyths.title: The Meeting of Ice and Fire
keyMyths.description: Where the cold of Niflheim met the heat of Muspelheim in Ginnungagap, the melting ice formed the giant Ymir. From Ymir's sweat arose the first giant race, and from the melting ice also came the cow Audhumla.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the Gods
keyMyths.description: Audhumla nourished herself by licking salty rime stones. Over three days, she revealed Buri, the ancestor of the gods. Buri's son Borr married Bestla, a giantess, and they had the gods Odin, Vili, and Vé.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Ymir
keyMyths.description: Odin and his brothers, Vili and Vé, slew Ymir, the first giant. From Ymir's body, they fashioned the world: his flesh became earth, his blood the sea, his bones the mountains, and his skull the sky.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humankind
keyMyths.description: After creating Midgard, Odin, Vili, and Vé found two logs on the shore. They gave them life: Odin gave them breath and life, Vili gave them wit and feeling, and Vé gave them form, speech, hearing, and sight, creating Ask and Embla, the first humans.

sources.name: Prose Edda
sources.description: Written by Snorri Sturluson in the 13th century, this is a key source for Norse mythology and cosmology.
sources.title: Prose Edda

sources.name: Poetic Edda
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, some of which predate the Prose Edda, offering alternative and often more archaic versions of the myths.
sources.title: Poetic Edda

sources.name: Gylfaginning
sources.description: The first book of Snorri Sturluson's Prose Edda, detailing the creation of the cosmos.
sources.title: Gylfaginning

sources.name: Völuspá
sources.description: A poem within the Poetic Edda that narrates the creation of the world and its eventual destruction (Ragnarök).
sources.title: Völuspá

sources.name: Rudolf Simek, Dictionary of Northern Mythology
sources.description: A modern academic resource providing analysis and interpretation of Norse mythological figures and concepts.
sources.title: Rudolf Simek, Dictionary of Northern Mythology

canonical: from ginnungagap to the first humans
